title: Lump-Sum (Basket) Purchase
event: Reyes Co. pays $400,000 cash for land and a building. Appraisals value the land at $180,000 and the building at $270,000. Prepare the entry.

lines.label: 400,000 × 40%
lines.why: Land takes its share of the PRICE PAID: 180/450 of appraisal weight.
lines.trap: Allocate the price paid, never the raw appraisals — you didn't pay 450,000.

lines.label: 400,000 × 60%
lines.why: Building takes 270/450 of the actual cost.
lines.trap: The split matters forever: buildings depreciate, land doesn't.

lines.label: total paid
lines.trap: Total debits must equal what was actually paid — 400,000, not the 450,000 appraisal sum.

memorize.body: Each asset's cost = total price × (its appraisal ÷ total appraisal).
Recording the raw appraisals books 50,000 of assets that were never bought — the classic basket-purchase error.

questions.prompt: Price $400,000; appraisals: land $180,000, building $270,000. Cost assigned to LAND = ?

distractors.feedback: That's the appraisal. Allocate the 400,000 actually PAID: 400,000 × 40% = 160,000.
A 50/50 split ignores the appraisals. The ratio is 180:270, i.e., 40%:60%.

build.scaffold: Percent each asset's appraisal of the total, then apply those percents to the price PAID.
